steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: This is a Boundaries question. The passage describes two minerals: krotite (transparent) and beryl (translucent). The blank falls between the clause about krotite and the phrase "Allowing only some light to pass through" — a participial phrase that modifies beryl. A sentence boundary is needed here so the participial phrase correctly attaches to beryl, not krotite.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'transparent. Allowing': A period ends the krotite sentence cleanly. "Allowing" (capitalized) begins a new sentence whose participial phrase correctly introduces and modifies beryl. This is the only option that creates a proper sentence boundary. ✓
  • Choice B — 'transparent allowing': No punctuation between the two clauses creates a run-on sentence. The participial phrase dangles without a clear boundary. ✗
  • Choice C — 'transparent, allowing': A comma alone between two independent clauses creates a comma splice. "All light passes through krotite" and the beryl clause are both independent, so a comma is insufficient. ✗
  • Choice D — 'transparent and allowing': The coordinating conjunction "and" tries to join two clauses that are not parallel in meaning. "Krotite is transparent and allowing only some light to pass through" is nonsensical — it makes both properties describe krotite rather than distinguishing the two minerals. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
